content:
Target split: four services along current domains.

orders-svc owns order lifecycle, order_items, order_state. Source of truth for order_id.
billing-svc owns payments, invoices, refunds. Source of truth for payment_id.
fulfillment-svc owns shipments, pick/pack, carrier handoff.
inventory-svc owns stock levels, reservations, SKU catalog read model.

Each service own Postgres schema. No cross-service table reads. No shared ORM models. Kafka topics per domain event stream. Redis per service for cache only, never shared state.

Order-to-payment strong consistency without 2PC: co-locate in orders-svc via outbox pattern.
Order placement writes order row + payment_intent outbox row in same Postgres tx.
Debezium or transactional outbox poller publishes to Kafka topic orders.payment_requested.
billing-svc consumes, charges, emits billing.payment_succeeded or billing.payment_failed.
orders-svc consumes billing event, transitions order state. Idempotency via (order_id, event_id) dedup table.
Saga compensates: payment_failed triggers order.cancel; inventory release via inventory.reservation_released event.

Sync vs async split:
Sync (gRPC or REST) only for read-path queries user blocks on: GET order detail, price quote, stock check at checkout. Timeouts 200ms, circuit breakers.
Async (Kafka) for all state transitions across services. At-least-once + idempotent consumers + versioned event schemas in schema registry.

Shared data rules:
One writer per entity. Others get read models built from events.
Inventory reservations: orders-svc calls inventory-svc sync reserve-stock RPC at checkout (blocking, needs immediate answer). Reservation has TTL. Confirmation on payment_succeeded, release on payment_failed or TTL expiry.
SKU catalog: inventory-svc publishes catalog.sku_updated, orders-svc caches locally.
Customer data: not split now — leave in orders-svc or extract later. Avoid splitting everything at once.

Migration 6 months, 8 eng:
Month 1: strangler fig scaffolding. Extract domain modules in monolith behind interface layer. Add outbox table, Kafka producers, schema registry. No new service yet.
Month 2: extract inventory-svc first (fewest sync deps). Dual-write period: monolith writes both old table and publishes events. Shadow read from new svc.
Month 3: cut inventory reads to new svc. Extract fulfillment-svc same pattern.
Month 4: extract billing-svc. This is the risky one — order-to-payment saga goes live. Run shadow mode 2 weeks before cutover.
Month 5: orders-svc becomes last remaining piece of monolith, rename + trim.
Month 6: harden, delete dual-write code, migrate remaining cron jobs, runbooks.

Deploy: one k8s namespace per service, separate Helm charts, independent CI. Per-service migration runner in init container, backwards-compatible migrations only (expand-contract). No multi-service migrations ever.

Testing: contract tests (Pact) between services on event schemas. Each service test suite under 3min. Integration env with real Kafka + Postgres per service.

Observability non-negotiable before extraction: OpenTelemetry traces across Kafka hops (trace context in event headers), per-service SLOs, saga state dashboard showing stuck orders.

Main risks: saga stuck states (build reaper + dead-letter topic + manual replay tool day one); event schema drift (registry + CI check); dual-write bugs during migration (reconciliation job comparing monolith vs new svc nightly).

orders-svc owns exclusively:
orders (order_id PK, customer_id FK-by-id-only, status, total_cents, currency, created_at, version)
order_items (order_item_id PK, order_id FK, sku, qty, unit_price_cents, line_total_cents)
order_events (append-only saga log, order_id, event_type, payload, occurred_at)
order_outbox (outbox_id PK, aggregate_id, event_type, payload, published_at)
idempotency_keys (key, order_id, response_hash, expires_at)
consumed_events (service_source, event_id, consumed_at) for dedup

orders-svc reads from others:
customer profile + addresses from customers-svc via sync gRPC GetCustomer at checkout, cached Redis 60s. Customer_id treated as opaque ref.
SKU price + availability from inventory-svc: catalog data via async subscription to catalog.sku_updated (local read model table sku_cache owned-as-cache, not source of truth). Stock reservation via sync gRPC ReserveStock, blocking, returns reservation_id with TTL.
Payment outcome from billing-svc via async consumption of billing.payment_succeeded / billing.payment_failed.
Shipment status from fulfillment-svc via async consumption of fulfillment.shipment_* events, projected to local read model shipment_status_projection for order-detail API.

orders-svc publishes:
orders.order_created
orders.payment_requested (saga trigger for billing)
orders.order_confirmed
orders.order_cancelled
orders.order_fulfillment_requested (saga trigger for fulfillment)

billing-svc owns exclusively:
payments (payment_id PK, order_id FK-by-id-only, amount_cents, currency, status, provider, provider_ref, created_at)
payment_attempts (attempt_id PK, payment_id, attempt_no, status, error_code, raw_response)
refunds (refund_id PK, payment_id, amount_cents, reason, status)
invoices (invoice_id PK, order_id-by-id, customer_id-by-id, issued_at, pdf_ref)
payment_outbox
consumed_events

billing-svc reads from others:
order amount + line items from orders.payment_requested event payload (self-contained, no callback).
Customer billing contact from customers-svc via sync gRPC GetBillingContact, cached.
No inventory or fulfillment reads.

billing-svc publishes:
billing.payment_succeeded (order_id, payment_id, amount)
billing.payment_failed (order_id, reason_code)
billing.refund_issued
billing.invoice_generated

inventory-svc owns exclusively:
skus (sku PK, name, attrs, active) — catalog source of truth
stock_levels (sku, warehouse_id, on_hand, available, reserved)
inventory_holds (hold_id PK, sku, qty, order_id-by-id, status, expires_at, created_at) — reservation records
stock_movements (append-only ledger of adjustments)
inventory_outbox
consumed_events

inventory-svc reads from others:
Order confirmation from orders.order_confirmed event to convert hold → committed deduction.
Order cancellation from orders.order_cancelled to release hold.
Shipment dispatched from fulfillment.shipment_dispatched to decrement on_hand.
No sync reads from others.

inventory-svc publishes:
catalog.sku_updated
catalog.sku_deactivated
inventory.hold_created
inventory.hold_released
inventory.hold_expired
inventory.stock_committed
inventory.low_stock_alert

fulfillment-svc owns exclusively:
shipments (shipment_id PK, order_id-by-id, status, carrier, tracking_no, shipped_at, delivered_at)
shipment_items (shipment_id, sku, qty) — snapshot from order at fulfillment time
pick_tasks (task_id PK, shipment_id, warehouse_id, picker_id, status)
pack_tasks
carrier_events (append-only log from carrier webhooks)
fulfillment_outbox
consumed_events

fulfillment-svc reads from others:
Trigger from orders.order_fulfillment_requested event carrying order_id + items + ship-to address snapshot (self-contained, no callback to orders).
Customer shipping contact not re-fetched — address snapshotted into event payload at order time to avoid temporal coupling.
No sync reads.

fulfillment-svc publishes:
fulfillment.shipment_created
fulfillment.shipment_picked
fulfillment.shipment_packed
fulfillment.shipment_dispatched
fulfillment.shipment_delivered
fulfillment.shipment_failed

customers-svc owns exclusively:
customers (customer_id PK, email, name, status, created_at)
customer_addresses (address_id PK, customer_id, type, line1, line2, city, region, postal, country)
customer_payment_methods (method_id PK, customer_id, provider_token_ref, last4, exp) — tokenized refs only, no PAN
customer_consents (customer_id, consent_type, granted_at)
customer_outbox

customers-svc reads from others: nothing. Leaf service.

customers-svc publishes:
customers.customer_created
customers.customer_updated
customers.address_updated
customers.payment_method_added
customers.payment_method_removed

Cross-cutting rules:
Foreign keys across service boundaries stored as bare IDs, no DB-level FK constraint. Referential integrity enforced by event contracts + saga compensation, not by database.
Every service owns its consumed_events dedup table and its outbox.
Event payloads self-contained enough that consumer never needs synchronous callback to producer for the same business transaction. Exception: reservation RPC (inventory) and customer lookup RPC (customers) are sync by design because caller blocks on user interaction.
Read models (sku_cache in orders-svc, shipment_status_projection in orders-svc) clearly marked as derived — rebuildable from event replay, never written to by application logic directly.
Snapshot-on-emit for volatile refs: shipping address copied into order_fulfillment_requested payload at emit time so later address edits don't retroactively change in-flight shipments.
One writer per table enforced by CI check scanning migrations for cross-service table names.

Saga type: orchestrated by orders-svc. Orders-svc holds saga state machine in orders.status column + order_events append log. No separate orchestrator service — orders-svc is orchestrator since it owns order aggregate.

State machine for order aggregate:

DRAFT → PENDING_INVENTORY → INVENTORY_HELD → PENDING_PAYMENT → PAID → PENDING_FULFILLMENT → FULFILLED → CLOSED

Terminal failure states: CANCELLED_INVENTORY_UNAVAILABLE, CANCELLED_PAYMENT_FAILED, CANCELLED_BY_USER, REFUNDED.

Compensation pseudo-states: COMPENSATING_PAYMENT, COMPENSATING_INVENTORY, COMPENSATING_FULFILLMENT.

Transitions (trigger → from → to → side effect):

client submits checkout → null → DRAFT → insert order row + items, idempotency_key check.
internal → DRAFT → PENDING_INVENTORY → sync gRPC ReserveStock(order_id, items, ttl=15min) to inventory-svc.
ReserveStock 200 → PENDING_INVENTORY → INVENTORY_HELD → store reservation_id + hold_expires_at.
ReserveStock 409 out-of-stock → PENDING_INVENTORY → CANCELLED_INVENTORY_UNAVAILABLE → emit orders.order_cancelled.
ReserveStock timeout/5xx → stay PENDING_INVENTORY → retry with exponential backoff + jitter, max 3. After max retries, move to CANCELLED_INVENTORY_UNAVAILABLE and emit inventory.hold_release_requested by order_id in case hold did land.
internal → INVENTORY_HELD → PENDING_PAYMENT → emit orders.payment_requested via outbox (order_id, amount, currency, customer_id, idempotency_key = order_id+attempt).
consume billing.payment_succeeded → PENDING_PAYMENT → PAID → record payment_id.
consume billing.payment_failed → PENDING_PAYMENT → COMPENSATING_INVENTORY → emit orders.order_cancelled (inventory-svc consumes, releases hold) → CANCELLED_PAYMENT_FAILED.
payment timeout (no billing event in 2× expected window, e.g. 5min) → PENDING_PAYMENT → stay, emit orders.payment_status_query via outbox; billing-svc replies with authoritative billing.payment_status event. If billing has no record, orders-svc retries orders.payment_requested with same idempotency_key (billing dedups). If retries exhausted OR hold_expires_at reached, transition to COMPENSATING_INVENTORY → CANCELLED_PAYMENT_FAILED.
internal → PAID → PENDING_FULFILLMENT → emit inventory.commit_hold_requested (by order_id) and orders.order_fulfillment_requested (with item + address snapshot).
consume fulfillment.shipment_dispatched (all items) → PENDING_FULFILLMENT → FULFILLED.
consume fulfillment.shipment_failed → PENDING_FULFILLMENT → COMPENSATING_FULFILLMENT → decide policy: retry dispatch OR refund.
internal → FULFILLED → CLOSED after delivery_confirmed.

Idempotency rules per step:

Every external call carries idempotency_key = order_id + saga_step + attempt_no. Receivers store (source_service, idempotency_key) and dedup.
Every inbound event stamped with event_id (UUID from producer outbox). Consumer writes to consumed_events(service_source, event_id) in same tx as state mutation. Duplicate event_id → no-op.
State transitions guarded by CAS on version column: UPDATE orders SET status=?, version=version+1 WHERE order_id=? AND version=? AND status IN (valid_from_states). Zero rows affected → reload + reevaluate, not error.
Transition handlers pure functions of (current_state, event). Re-running same event against post-transition state is no-op because guard fails.
Outbox insert + state update in same Postgres tx. Publisher is separate process; at-least-once to Kafka. Consumers dedup on event_id.

Timeout handling:

Every non-terminal state has timeout_deadline column. Background sweeper job (single worker, leader-elected via Postgres advisory lock on hash of saga_sweeper, not a distributed lock on business data) scans orders WHERE status IN (non_terminal) AND timeout_deadline < now() every 30s, LIMIT 500, FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED.
PENDING_INVENTORY timeout 30s → retry or fail.
INVENTORY_HELD + not transitioned within 10s → auto-advance to PENDING_PAYMENT (normally instant via outbox, this is safety net).
PENDING_PAYMENT timeout 5min → emit payment_status_query.
PENDING_PAYMENT hard deadline = hold_expires_at − 1min → force COMPENSATING_INVENTORY.
COMPENSATING_* timeout 10min → escalate to dead-letter + ops alert, no auto-retry beyond N=5.

Critical failure scenarios:

(a) Payment timeout. Billing-svc processing slow or event lost.
orders-svc at t+5min: emit payment_status_query, billing-svc answers with current authoritative status. Three cases.
billing has payment_succeeded → orders-svc re-consumes own emitted reply, transitions to PAID. Idempotent because consumed_events dedups if already applied.
billing has payment_failed → compensate inventory, cancel.
billing has no record → orders-svc republishes orders.payment_requested with same idempotency_key. Billing either picks up fresh or dedups if duplicate.
Hard cutoff at hold_expires_at − 1min: force cancel regardless. Prevents stock starvation.

(b) Inventory released after payment. Hold TTL expired but payment then succeeded (race).
Detection: when processing billing.payment_succeeded, orders-svc checks hold_expires_at. If now > hold_expires_at, transition to PAID_UNRESERVED pseudo-state → emit inventory.rereserve_requested. inventory-svc attempts reservation anew.
If rereserve succeeds → PENDING_FULFILLMENT as normal.
If rereserve fails (stock gone) → transition to PAID_BACKORDER. Options: (1) wait for restock with customer-visible ETA, (2) partial fulfill what is available, (3) refund via billing.refund_requested → REFUNDED.
Key rule: never auto-cancel an order that has PAID state without explicit refund saga. Money moved, so compensation must move money back.
Mitigation: set hold TTL generously longer than p99 payment latency + safety margin (e.g. 15min hold vs 30s p99 payment).

(c) Partial fulfillment rollback. Order has 3 items, 2 ship, 1 fails permanently at warehouse.
fulfillment-svc emits fulfillment.shipment_dispatched per shipment + fulfillment.item_unfulfillable(order_id, sku, qty, reason).
orders-svc consumes item_unfulfillable → transition to PARTIALLY_FULFILLED pseudo-state. Computes refund_amount = unfulfilled line totals + prorated tax/shipping.
Emits billing.partial_refund_requested(order_id, amount, reason, idempotency_key).
billing-svc processes refund idempotently (idempotency_key dedups), emits billing.refund_issued.
orders-svc on refund_issued → if all shipped items delivered → FULFILLED_WITH_REFUND terminal. Else remain PARTIALLY_FULFILLED until deliveries confirmed.
Also emits inventory.release_unfulfilled (for the 1 item whose hold was committed but not shipped) → inventory-svc increments on_hand back.
Never try to claw back already-dispatched items via saga. Customer-initiated returns are separate flow.

Concurrency without distributed locks:

Single-writer per aggregate: only orders-svc mutates order row. Within orders-svc, CAS on version column gives optimistic concurrency — no row locks held across I/O.
Kafka partition key = order_id. One partition consumer processes events for a given order in order, eliminating intra-order concurrency.
Inventory hold uniqueness: inventory-svc enforces unique(order_id, status='active') constraint on inventory_holds. Duplicate ReserveStock with same order_id returns existing hold_id (idempotent).
Payment uniqueness: billing-svc enforces unique(idempotency_key) on payments. Duplicate orders.payment_requested returns existing payment outcome, no double-charge.

Failure observability:

saga_state_duration histogram per state — alert on p99 in non-terminal state > SLO.
stuck_sagas gauge = count(orders WHERE status IN non_terminal AND updated_at < now() − 1h) — pages ops.
compensation_rate counter per compensation type — trend indicates systemic issues.
dead-letter topics per service with replay tool. Ops can inspect payload + requeue after fix.

Non-goals by design:
No cross-service transactions.
No row-level locks held across RPC boundaries.
No synchronous fan-out to multiple services; every cross-service write is async via outbox+Kafka. Only sync calls are ReserveStock (pre-commit) and GetCustomer (read-only).
No reliance on clock sync — all ordering within aggregate via version column, across services via Kafka partition order.

Eventual-consistency windows ranked by user-visible impact.

Window 1: order appears in "my orders" list after checkout.
Gap: user clicks Place Order → orders-svc commits DRAFT/PENDING_PAYMENT → redirect to order-history page. If history page reads from a projection fed by Kafka, lag = outbox poll + Kafka + consumer apply ≈ 50-500ms typical, seconds under load.
User expectation: order visible immediately on redirect.
Mitigation: read-your-writes via primary read. Post-checkout redirect carries order_id; order-detail page reads orders-svc primary DB directly (not projection). History list page uses projection but primes with recent-order cache keyed by session: on checkout success, client stashes order_id + summary in localStorage and overlays it onto history list until projection catches up.
Bound: order_detail reachable within 100ms of commit. History list reflects new order within 1s p99. UI shows "Processing" chip until PAID.

Window 2: payment status transition (PENDING_PAYMENT → PAID) reaching the order detail page.
Gap: billing-svc completes charge, publishes payment_succeeded, orders-svc consumer applies 100-800ms later. User refreshing page sees PENDING longer than reality.
User expectation: once bank/3DS confirms, page shows Paid.
Mitigation: two-layer.
Layer A: after redirect from payment provider, client polls GET /orders/{id}/status with ETag every 1s for up to 10s, then backs off to 5s. Endpoint reads orders-svc primary.
Layer B: server-sent events or WebSocket channel from orders-svc pushes state transitions to subscribed client. Fallback to polling if SSE fails.
Bound: PAID state visible to user within 2s p95, 5s p99 after provider callback. UI shows explicit "Confirming payment..." with spinner, not a stale PENDING.

Window 3: inventory availability shown on product page vs actual hold-eligible stock.
Gap: product page reads inventory projection (async from stock_movements). A burst of orders can deplete stock before projection updates. User sees "In stock" → checkout → ReserveStock 409.
User expectation: if page says available, checkout should succeed.
Mitigation: never trust projection for checkout decision. Projection drives display only. Checkout always hits inventory-svc ReserveStock sync. On 409, UI shows "this item just sold out" with suggestion to remove + continue. Display-side: show "Low stock" threshold generously (e.g. show "Low stock" when available<10 even if projection shows 15) to hedge staleness. For high-velocity items, invalidate product-page cache on inventory.hold_created events instead of polling.
Bound: projection lag ≤ 2s p99. Acceptable because final decision is sync. UI must never promise availability, only indicate.

Window 4: sku catalog update (price change, rename, deactivation) reaching orders-svc local read model.
Gap: inventory-svc publishes catalog.sku_updated. orders-svc sku_cache lags. User sees old price during checkout.
User expectation: price at checkout must match price at order submit. Price must not change mid-flow.
Mitigation: orders-svc snapshots unit_price_cents into order_items at DRAFT creation — never re-reads sku_cache after. If staleness is discovered pre-commit (ReserveStock returns current catalog version), orders-svc compares snapshot vs authoritative price from inventory-svc response and, if delta > 0, aborts with "price changed, please review" UI flow. Sku deactivation: checkout rejects deactivated sku at ReserveStock time.
Bound: catalog propagation lag ≤ 5s p99. User never sees mid-checkout price change; they see either the snapshotted price or an explicit re-confirm dialog.

Window 5: shipment status projection in orders-svc vs fulfillment-svc truth.
Gap: fulfillment.shipment_dispatched published, orders-svc projection lags. Customer refreshes order page, sees "preparing" when it is actually "shipped with tracking".
User expectation: tracking number available once carrier picks up.
Mitigation: order detail page hybrid read. Core order fields (status, items, totals) from orders-svc primary. Shipment block either (a) read from orders-svc projection for list views and (b) optionally call fulfillment-svc GET /shipments?order_id= directly for detail view to bypass projection lag. Cache fulfillment-svc response 30s client-side. Push shipment_dispatched updates over SSE channel (same as payment).
Bound: tracking number visible within 3s p95 of carrier handoff. UI shows "shipped, tracking pending" state so user is not confused during the window.

Window 6: inventory release after order cancellation.
Gap: user cancels order, orders-svc transitions to CANCELLED, emits event, inventory-svc releases hold async. Another user trying to order same item sees "out of stock" until release propagates.
User expectation: cancelled item should be available to others reasonably quickly, but this is a second-user problem, not first-user. Low user-visible severity.
Mitigation: accept the lag. Hold release event processed <1s typical. No UI affordance needed.
Bound: ≤ 2s p99.

Window 7: refund reflected in order + payment provider statement.
Gap: partial_refund_requested → billing-svc processes → billing.refund_issued → orders-svc projects. User sees "refund pending" until event propagates, and bank statement updates on provider schedule (days).
User expectation: order page shows refund status quickly; money arrives when bank says so.
Mitigation: orders-svc projects refund_status=requested immediately on emit (optimistic projection in same tx as state transition), updates to issued on event. UI shows explicit "refund issued, 3-5 business days to appear" copy. Do not promise immediate money return.
Bound: refund_status=issued visible within 2s p95 of billing event. Bank appearance delay is out of scope but must be communicated.

Window 8: customer profile update (new address, new card) used by in-flight order.
Gap: customer updates shipping address at t0. Order placed at t0+100ms snapshots new address into order_fulfillment_requested? Or uses cached customer record from pre-update?
User expectation: "I updated my address before placing this order" should work.
Mitigation: address selection at checkout is explicit — user picks address from customers-svc list at checkout time, sync gRPC, not cached. Selected address_id + full snapshot embedded in order_items context and in fulfillment event. Never resolve address_id at fulfillment time.
Bound: n/a — sync read eliminates window.

Window 9: search / list pages (admin dashboards, CS tools) backed by derived read stores.
Gap: CS rep looks up order by email 2s after customer calls — may not be in search index yet.
User expectation: CS can find any recent order.
Mitigation: CS tool has two-pane lookup. Primary pane = ElasticSearch projection (fast, eventual). Fallback pane = direct orders-svc API by order_id or customer_id (authoritative, slower, scoped). CS rep sees "as of N seconds ago" timestamp on search pane so staleness is transparent.
Bound: search index lag ≤ 10s p99. Authoritative lookup always available.

Window 10: analytics / finance reconciliation.
Gap: end-of-day revenue report needs all PAID orders consistent with billing totals. Stream lag could misstate day boundaries.
User expectation: finance numbers are correct.
Mitigation: reports run on T+1 snapshot, not live stream. Reconcile orders-svc PAID totals vs billing-svc payment_succeeded totals daily, alert on mismatch > 0.01%. Live dashboards clearly labeled "near real time" with last-refresh timestamp.
Bound: daily reconciliation tolerance 0 rows mismatched after 24h. Live dashboards ≤ 60s lag.

Cross-cutting UI principles.

Always render authoritative state on the page the user lands on post-action. Never redirect from a write to a projection-read without a read-your-writes guarantee.
Use explicit transient states in UI (Processing, Confirming, Preparing) rather than letting stale projections masquerade as final states.
Expose "as of" timestamps on any view backed by an async projection so users know it can lag.
Push state transitions over SSE/WebSocket where available; fall back to polling with backoff.
Snapshot volatile references (price, address) at decision time into the event payload; never re-resolve at consumer time.
Never let async projections make checkout/payment decisions — only display decisions.

SLO summary (p95 unless noted):
order visible to self: 100ms primary, 1s projection.
payment state to UI: 2s.
inventory display staleness: 2s.
catalog propagation: 5s.
shipment status to order page: 3s.
cancellation inventory release: 2s.
refund status projection: 2s.
CS search index: 10s.
finance reconciliation: T+1.

Non-mitigable windows acknowledged: bank settlement delay (days), carrier tracking first scan (hours) — communicated in UI copy, not engineered around.

We splitting our orders platform from one large codebase into four focused services — orders, billing, fulfillment, inventory — plus a small customers service. Today all four domains live in one Django application, which means every team's changes collide in the same release, every deploy waits on everyone else's database migration, and our test suite takes 12 minutes to confirm nothing broke. Splitting lets each of our four sub-teams ship independently, cuts per-service test time to under 3 minutes, and removes migrations as a deploy bottleneck. Critically, we keep strict correctness on the money path: when a customer places an order, inventory is held, payment is charged, and order state updates in lockstep via a well-known reliable messaging pattern (outbox + Kafka + saga), not fragile distributed transactions. Customer-visible behavior is unchanged; the goal is faster, safer delivery, not new features in this phase.

Timeline 6 months, phased to de-risk. Months 1-2 build the plumbing and extract inventory (lowest risk, most isolated). Months 3-4 extract fulfillment and billing — billing being the highest-stakes cut because it touches payments; we run it in shadow mode for two weeks before flipping real traffic. Months 5-6 finalize the orders service, delete old code, harden monitoring. Top risks: (1) dual-write bugs during migration — mitigated by nightly reconciliation jobs comparing old vs new; (2) stuck orders from cross-service message delays — mitigated by timeout sweepers, dead-letter queues, and an ops replay tool built day one; (3) short-term delivery slowdown as the team learns new patterns — mitigated by sequencing extractions from lowest to highest risk so the team builds muscle before the billing cutover. The 8-engineer team stays fully allocated for the 6 months; we expect feature velocity on orders to dip ~20% during months 3-4 and recover and exceed baseline by month 7 once release coupling is gone.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: day1_split_health_observable
C: five_slos_defined ∧ alert_thresholds_set ∧ canary_vs_control_query ∧ dashboard_groupings ∧ no_2pc ∧ no_distributed_locks

G: audit_jwt_module_rank_severity
C: pyjwt_api ∧ hs256_rs256_shared_verify ∧ header_alg_trusted
P: alg_none_allowed_critical ∧ algorithm_confusion_hs_rs_critical ∧ hardcoded_dev_secret_critical ∧ exp_verification_disabled_high ∧ weak_symmetric_secret_high ∧ no_iss_aud_nbf_checks_medium ∧ no_kid_or_key_rotation_medium ∧ role_default_user_fallback_medium ∧ no_signature_required_claims_medium ∧ unverified_header_drives_algo_low_informational
V: remove_none_from_algos ∧ split_hs_and_rs_keys_per_alg ∧ load_secret_from_env_or_kms ∧ enable_verify_exp_and_leeway ∧ enforce_iss_aud_and_required_claims ∧ pin_single_algorithm_per_key ∧ drop_role_default ∧ add_kid_and_rotation
A: pyjwt_decode("algorithms=['HS256']_only") ∧ remove("'none'_from_ALGOS") ∧ secret_from_env("JWT_SECRET") ∧ options("{'require':['exp','iss','aud','sub'],'verify_exp':True}") ∧ decode_kwargs("issuer=...,audience=...,leeway=30") ∧ select_key_by_header_kid ∧ raise_on_missing_role ∧ rotate_and_revoke_dev_secret

1. alg=none bypass — trivial, no secret needed

Attacker forges token with unsigned alg:
header: {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}
payload: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
signature: empty

Full token: eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.

"none" sits in ALGOS, so jwt.decode accepts unsigned token. require_admin passes. Full account takeover, zero prior knowledge.

2. HS/RS algorithm confusion — needs public key, still devastating

If RS256 used in prod, public key often exposed (jwks endpoint, repo, cert). Attacker signs HS256 token using RSA public key as HMAC secret.

header: {"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}
payload: {"sub":"admin","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
signature: HMAC-SHA256(rsa_public_key_pem, header_b64 + "." + payload_b64)

jwt.decode gets algorithms=['HS256','RS256','none'], picks HS256 per header, verifies HMAC against SECRET. Here SECRET is hardcoded, so attacker only needs repo access — but even with proper RSA rotation, passing both HS and RS in algorithms list enables this class. Classic CVE-2016-10555 shape.

3. Expired token replay — exp check disabled

options={'verify_exp': False} means stolen token never expires from library's view.

header: {"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}
payload: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":1600000000}  # 2020
signature: valid HMAC with leaked dev secret

Token from logs, old backup, stale session, cached response — all still valid forever. Combined with hardcoded 'development-secret-do-not-commit' in source, attacker with repo read (past contributor, leaked git history, open-source fork) mints permanent admin tokens offline.

Exploitability order reflects prerequisites: none bypass needs nothing, confusion needs public key, replay needs either secret leak or stolen token.

G: fix_jwt_service_with_grandfather_window

P: remove_none_and_rs256_from_accept_list ∧ load_secret_from_env ∧ enforce_exp_iss_aud_required ∧ gate_legacy_path_by_iat_within_30d ∧ legacy_tolerates_missing_iss_aud_but_still_verifies_sig_and_exp ∧ new_tokens_include_iat_iss_aud_and_kid
V: alg_none_rejected ∧ rs256_rejected_in_hs_path ∧ decode_uses_algorithms_hs256_only ∧ expired_tokens_rejected_outside_grandfather ∧ tokens_older_than_30d_without_iss_aud_rejected ∧ verify_returns_token_same_shape
A: replace_module("auth/jwt_service.py") ∧ code("
import os, time, jwt
from dataclasses import dataclass

SECRET = os.environ['JWT_SECRET']
ISSUER = os.environ.get('JWT_ISS', 'sigil')
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get('JWT_AUD', 'sigil-api')
ALGO = 'HS256'
GRANDFATHER_SECONDS = 30 * 24 * 3600
LEEWAY = 30

@dataclass
class Token:
    sub: str
    exp: int
    role: str

def sign(sub: str, role: str, ttl: int = 3600) -> str:
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        'sub': sub,
        'role': role,
        'iat': now,
        'nbf': now,
        'exp': now + ttl,
        'iss': ISSUER,
        'aud': AUDIENCE,
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm=ALGO, headers={'kid': 'v1'})

def _decode_strict(token: str) -> dict:
    return jwt.decode(
        token, SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],
        issuer=ISSUER,
        audience=AUDIENCE,
        leeway=LEEWAY,
        options={'require': ['exp', 'iat', 'iss', 'aud', 'sub']},
    )

def _decode_legacy(token: str) -> dict:
    payload = jwt.decode(
        token, SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],
        leeway=LEEWAY,
        options={'require': ['exp', 'sub'], 'verify_aud': False, 'verify_iss': False},
    )
    iat = payload.get('iat')
    exp = payload.get('exp', 0)
    anchor = iat if iat is not None else exp - 3600
    if int(time.time()) - int(anchor) > GRANDFATHER_SECONDS:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('legacy token outside grandfather window')
    return payload

def verify(token: str) -> Token:
    header = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)
    if header.get('alg') != ALGO:
        raise jwt.InvalidAlgorithmError('algorithm not allowed')
    try:
        payload = _decode_strict(token)
    except jwt.MissingRequiredClaimError:
        payload = _decode_legacy(token)
    role = payload.get('role')
    if not role:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError('missing role')
    return Token(sub=payload['sub'], exp=int(payload['exp']), role=role)

def require_admin(token_str: str) -> Token:
    t = verify(token_str)
    if t.role != 'admin':
        raise PermissionError('not admin')
    return t
") ∧ env_requires("JWT_SECRET,JWT_ISS,JWT_AUD") ∧ rotate_secret_before_deploy ∧ remove_hardcoded_dev_secret ∧ plan_cutover_after("30d")

@flint v0 hybrid
G: write_pytest_regression_tests_for_jwt_service
C: pytest ∧ pyjwt ∧ fixed_env_secret_iss_aud ∧ covers_five_attack_classes
P: alg_none_must_raise ∧ alg_rs256_must_raise ∧ expired_token_past_grandfather_must_raise ∧ forged_sig_wrong_secret_must_raise ∧ role_spoof_via_tampered_payload_must_raise ∧ happy_path_admin_accepted
V: all_tests_raise_invalid_token_or_permission_error ∧ happy_path_returns_token_with_role_admin ∧ no_test_depends_on_network
A: create_file("tests/test_jwt_service.py") ∧ code("
import os, time, base64, json, hmac, hashlib
import pytest

os.environ['JWT_SECRET'] = 'unit-test-secret-32bytes-minimum-xx'
os.environ['JWT_ISS'] = 'sigil'
os.environ['JWT_AUD'] = 'sigil-api'

import jwt as pyjwt
from auth import jwt_service as svc


def _b64(obj) -> str:
    raw = json.dumps(obj, separators=(',', ':')).encode()
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(raw).rstrip(b'=').decode()


def _good_payload(role='admin', **over):
    now = int(time.time())
    p = {
        'sub': 'u1', 'role': role, 'iat': now, 'nbf': now,
        'exp': now + 3600, 'iss': 'sigil', 'aud': 'sigil-api',
    }
    p.update(over)
    return p


def test_happy_path_admin():
    tok = svc.sign('u1', 'admin')
    out = svc.verify(tok)
    assert out.sub == 'u1'
    assert out.role == 'admin'


def test_alg_none_rejected():
    header = _b64({'alg': 'none', 'typ': 'JWT'})
    payload = _b64(_good_payload())
    forged = f'{header}.{payload}.'
    with pytest.raises((pyjwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, pyjwt.InvalidTokenError, ValueError)):
        svc.verify(forged)


def test_alg_rs256_rejected():
    header = _b64({'alg': 'RS256', 'typ': 'JWT'})
    payload = _b64(_good_payload())
    forged = f'{header}.{payload}.deadbeef'
    with pytest.raises((pyjwt.InvalidAlgorithmError, pyjwt.InvalidTokenError, ValueError)):
        svc.verify(forged)


def test_expired_token_outside_grandfather_rejected():
    long_ago = int(time.time()) - (60 * 24 * 3600)
    payload = _good_payload(iat=long_ago, nbf=long_ago, exp=long_ago + 3600)
    tok = pyjwt.encode(payload, os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256')
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_expired_within_grandfather_still_rejected_on_exp():
    recent = int(time.time()) - (5 * 24 * 3600)
    payload = _good_payload(iat=recent, nbf=recent, exp=recent + 60)
    tok = pyjwt.encode(payload, os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256')
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_forged_signature_rejected():
    payload = _good_payload()
    tok = pyjwt.encode(payload, 'attacker-guessed-secret', algorithm='HS256')
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tok)


def test_role_spoof_via_payload_tamper_rejected():
    tok = svc.sign('u1', 'user')
    header_b64, payload_b64, sig_b64 = tok.split('.')
    raw = base64.urlsafe_b64decode(payload_b64 + '==')
    data = json.loads(raw)
    data['role'] = 'admin'
    tampered_payload = _b64(data)
    tampered = f'{header_b64}.{tampered_payload}.{sig_b64}'
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tampered)


def test_require_admin_rejects_user_role():
    tok = svc.sign('u1', 'user')
    with pytest.raises(PermissionError):
        svc.require_admin(tok)


def test_legacy_token_without_iss_aud_accepted_within_window():
    now = int(time.time())
    legacy = {'sub': 'u1', 'role': 'user', 'iat': now - 10 * 24 * 3600,
              'exp': now + 3600}
    tok = pyjwt.encode(legacy, os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256')
    out = svc.verify(tok)
    assert out.role == 'user'


def test_legacy_token_outside_window_rejected():
    now = int(time.time())
    legacy = {'sub': 'u1', 'role': 'user', 'iat': now - 40 * 24 * 3600,
              'exp': now + 3600}
    tok = pyjwt.encode(legacy, os.environ['JWT_SECRET'], algorithm='HS256')
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(tok)
") ∧ run_cmd("pytest tests/test_jwt_service.py -q")

Security review of our authentication service uncovered three serious flaws in how user login tokens get verified. Most severe: system currently accepts tokens marked as unsigned, meaning anyone on internet can hand-craft credentials claiming to be any user, including administrators, without needing any secret or password. Second: same shared secret used to sign tokens sits in our source code, labeled as development-only, and was never rotated. Third: expiration checks disabled, so tokens that should have lapsed remain valid indefinitely. Combined impact: full administrative takeover achievable by unauthenticated external attacker with near-zero skill.

Business risk sits at highest tier. Any account — customer, internal staff, admin — can be impersonated. That exposes customer data, billing records, and any privileged action administrators can perform. Because exploit requires no credential theft and leaves weak audit trail, we cannot assume absence of past abuse; we can only confirm it did not leave obvious traces. For regulated customer segments (enterprise contracts with security addenda, any EU personal data, payment flows) this qualifies as material control failure.

Urgency: deploy fix this week, not next sprint. Patched module already drafted and tested; it closes all three flaws, rotates secret via environment configuration, and keeps existing user sessions working for 30 days so customers experience no forced logout. Recommended sequence: rotate secret in production today, deploy patched verifier within 48 hours, monitor auth logs for anomalies during grandfather window, retire legacy path after 30 days.

Disclosure obligations likely apply and should be assessed by legal and compliance before end of week. Under GDPR, personal-data breach notification to supervisory authority within 72 hours triggers if we conclude unauthorized access was likely, not only confirmed. Enterprise contracts with security-incident clauses may impose shorter windows and direct customer notice. Recommend parallel track: engineering ships fix, legal reviews log evidence and contract language, communications prepares holding statement in case outside inquiry arrives before our assessment concludes.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_checkout_500_outage
C: pool_size_20 ∧ reaper_scans_8m_rows ∧ recurrence_every_23min ∧ errors_coincide_with_reaper_start
P: reaper_holds_long_txn ∧ reaper_saturates_pool ∧ checkout_threads_wait_5000ms ∧ pool_exhaustion_cascades_500s
V: recovery_follows_reaper_finish ∧ p99_drops_after_"reaped in 4.3s" ∧ three_incidents_align_with_scheduler_1
A: shrink_reaper_batch ∧ isolate_reaper_pool ∧ reduce_txn_scope ∧ stagger_schedule_off_peak

Reaper and checkout share same connection pool of 20.

Reaper scans ~8M rows to find expired sessions.

That scan runs inside one long transaction holding one or more connections.

Under load, checkout already uses most of pool; reaper arrives, grabs connection, issues heavy query.

Heavy query locks or slows rows/pages that checkout also touches (likely sessions table, same one checkout reads/writes).

Checkout transactions now block on locks or wait longer per query, so connections stay checked out longer.

Waiting queue grows past pool capacity, acquire times out at 5000ms, handler throws RollbackException because transaction never actually started.

Pattern repeats every ~23 min because that is reaper cadence; each run reproduces exhaustion until reap finishes in ~4s and pool drains.

Root cause: reaper competes with live traffic for same pool and same hot table, turning routine maintenance into synchronized pool starvation.

Not a capacity bug. Scheduling and isolation bug.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: prevent_pool_starvation_during_reap
C: shared_pool_size_20 ∧ reaper_single_txn_8m_rows ∧ checkout_contends_same_table ∧ cannot_delay_reaper
P: isolate_reaper_on_dedicated_pool ∧ batch_delete_in_chunks ∧ short_txn_per_chunk ∧ yield_between_chunks
V: checkout_pool_unaffected_during_reap ∧ no_acquire_timeouts ∧ p99_stable_at_reap_start ∧ reaper_still_completes_each_cycle
A: add_reaper_datasource("pool_size=2") ∧ replace_single_delete_with("DELETE ... WHERE expired LIMIT 5000") ∧ loop_until_zero_rows ∧ sleep("50ms")_between_batches ∧ index_on("sessions.expires_at")

On night of April 19, between 22:04 and 22:50 UTC, our checkout API experienced three short windows of elevated errors on /api/checkout. Each window lasted roughly 5 to 8 seconds, with one longer episode of about 23 minutes during which a subset of checkout attempts returned 500 errors or timed out. Requests outside these windows completed normally, and no payment data was lost, duplicated, or incorrectly charged.

Impact was limited to checkout attempts made during those brief windows. Customers who hit an error would have seen failed submission and been prompted to retry; retries after each window succeeded. We estimate low single-digit percentage of checkout attempts during incident period were affected. No accounts, orders, or payment details were exposed or corrupted.

Root cause: background maintenance job that cleans up expired user sessions was sharing same database connection pool as live checkout traffic. When that job ran, it scanned millions of session rows in one large operation and temporarily consumed enough database capacity that checkout requests could not acquire connection quickly enough, causing them to time out. Job itself was working correctly and doing necessary work; problem was that it competed with customer traffic for same limited resources instead of running in isolation.

We have already deployed fix that gives cleanup job its own small, dedicated pool of database connections, so it can never again starve checkout of capacity. We also changed job to delete expired sessions in small batches with brief pauses between them, rather than one heavy pass, which keeps database responsive throughout. An index was added to speed up scan so each batch completes in milliseconds.

Going forward, we are auditing other scheduled jobs for similar shared-pool patterns and adding alerts that page on-call engineer at first sign of connection-pool saturation, rather than after customer impact begins. We apologize for disruption and appreciate your patience. If you believe specific order was affected and has not recovered on retry, please reach out to support and we will resolve it directly.

Honest read: we had enough information to predict this, and we did not connect dots. Reaper was written months ago when session table was small, sized for tens of thousands of rows, not eight million. Nobody revisited its assumptions as table grew, because it kept finishing and nobody got paged. We rely heavily on "did it error" as signal of health, and this job never errored — it just got heavier and heavier until one night its footprint overlapped with peak checkout traffic. A process that treated growth of backing tables as review trigger, rather than waiting for incident, would have surfaced this. Something as light as quarterly review of top-N largest tables and jobs that touch them would likely have caught it.

Second gap: we do not load-test background jobs against realistic production-scale data. Staging session table has maybe 50k rows, so reaper completes in 100ms there and looks invisible. Any test that exercised reaper against a table sized like prod, concurrent with synthetic checkout load, would have shown pool contention immediately. This is not expensive to build; it is mostly a cultural gap — we treat background jobs as "fire and forget" rather than as first-class production workloads. Worth investing in shared harness that replays prod-scale fixtures against scheduled jobs under load.

Third, and most uncomfortable: shared connection pool between request path and batch work is pattern we have known was risky for a while, and we carried it anyway because isolating pools felt like yak-shave with no incident to justify cost. Incident now justifies cost, which is fine, but it means our prioritization was reactive. Healthier posture would be to maintain small running list of "known structural risks we have not paid down yet" that gets reviewed each planning cycle, so things like this get weighed explicitly rather than forgotten until they bite. Not about blame — about making latent risk visible enough that deferring it is deliberate choice, not drift.
